english_head: You have seen the performance of the man fighting in armour, Nicias and Laches; but my friend Melesias and I did not tell you at the time our reason for requesting you to come and see it with us. However, we will tell you now; for we think we should speak our minds freely to friends like you. Some people, of course, pour ridicule on such appeals, and when consulted for their advice will not say what they think,

greek_head: τις αὐτοῖς συμβουλεύσηται οὐκ ἂν εἴποιεν ἃ νοοῦσιν ἀλλὰ στοχαζόμενοι τοῦ συμβουλευομένου ἄλλα λέγουσι παρὰ τὴν

But you, we consider, not merely have the necessary discernment but will give us the benefit of it in telling us just what is in your minds and hence we have enlisted your counsel on the question which we are about to lay before you. Now the matter about which I have made all this long preamble is this:

english_head: That is the question which we wanted to discuss with you. And we look to you now, on your part, to give us your advice, first as to whether you think this accomplishment should be learnt or not, and then as to any other such art or pursuit that you can recommend for a young man; and also, how you feel inclined as regards our partnership. For myself, Lysimachus and Melesias, I highly approve of your purpose, and am ready to lend a hand and I may say the same, I think, for Laches here.

english_head: Yes, you think truly, Nicias. For that remark which Lysimachus made just now about his father and the father of Melesias was very apposite, in my opinion, not only to them but to us and to all who deal with public affairs: it is practically the rule with them, as he says, to treat their private concerns, whether connected with children or any thing else, in a slighting, careless spirit. You are quite right in saying that, Lysimachus;

Well then, I take it to be a certain

greek_tokens: o ge dia pas peri andreia fuw deomai eipon alla meis deomai ei ge o erwtaw apokrinw egw autos outos toinun egw fainw outi pas ge ws oimh karteria andreia su fainw tekmairomai de enqende sxedon gar tis oida eimi laxh ostis o panu kalh pragma hgeomai su andreia eimi eu men oun eimi ostis o kalos oukoun o men meta fronhsis karteria kalos agaqos panu ge
english_head: Why, of course we must, if we are each to answer the other’s actual question. Now it appears to me that by no means all endurance, as I conceive it, can appear to you to be courage. And my grounds for thinking so are these: I am almost certain, Laches, that you rank courage among the nobler qualities. Nay, among the noblest, you may be quite certain. And endurance joined with wisdom is noble and good? Very much so.

greek_head: Τί δ' ἡ μετ' ἀφροσύνης οὐ τοὐναντίον ταύτῃ βλαβερὰ καὶ κακοῦργος
greek_tokens: tis de o meta afrosunh ou enantion outos blaberos kai kakourgos nai kalos oun tis fhmi su eimi o toioutos eimi kakourgos te kai blaberos oukoun dikaios ge eimi swkraths ou ara o ge toioutos karteria andreia omologhsis eimi epei ou kalos eimi o de andreia kalos eimi alhqhs legw o fronimos ara karteria kata o sos logos andreia an eaw eoika
english_head: But what of it when joined with folly? Is it not, on the contrary, hurtful and mischievous? Yes. And can you say that such a thing is noble, when it is both mischievous and hurtful? Not with any justice, Socrates. Then you will not admit that such an endurance is courage, seeing that it is not noble, whereas courage is a noble quality. That is true. So, by your account, wise endurance will be courage. Apparently.

greek_head: Ἴδωμεν δή ἡ εἰς τί φρόνιμος ἢ ἡ εἰς ἅπαντα καὶ τὰ μεγάλα καὶ τὰ σμικρά οἷον εἴ τις καρτερεῖ ἀναλίσκων
greek_tokens: eidon dh o eis tis fronimos h o eis apas kai o megas kai o mikros oios ei tis karterew analiskw argurion fronimos eidoi ostis analiskw pleos kteomai outos andreios kalew an ma diw ou egw alla oios ei tis iatros eimi peripleumonia o uios exw h allos tis kai deomai pinw h esqiw
english_head: Now let us see in what it is wise. In all things, whether great or small? For instance, if a man endures in spending money wisely, because he knows that by spending he will gain more, would you call him courageous? On my word, not I. Or what do you call it in the case of a doctor who, when his son or anyone else is suffering from inflammation of the lungs and begs for something to drink or eat, inflexibly and enduringly refuses?

greek_head: δοῦναι μὴ κάμπτοιτο ἀλλὰ καρτεροῖ Οὐδ' ὁπωστιοῦν οὐδ' αὕτη
greek_tokens: didwmi mh kamptw alla karterew oude opwsoun oude outos alla en polemos karterew anhr kai eqelw maxomai fronimos logizomai oida men ostis bohqew allos autos pros elasswn de kai faulos maxomai h meqo os autos eimi eti de xwrion exis kreisswn outos o meta o toioutos fronhsis kai paraskeuazw karterew andreios an fhmi h o en o enantios stratopedon eqelw upomenw te kai karterew
english_head: That is no case of it, in any sense, either. Well now, when a man endures in war, and is willing to fight, on a wise calculation whereby he knows that others will come to his aid, and that the forces against him will be fewer and feebler than those who are with him, and when he has besides the advantage of position,—would you say of this man, if he endures with such wisdom and preparation, that he, or a man in the opposing army who is willing to stand up against him and endure, is the more coura

greek_head: Τὸν ἐν τῷ ἐναντίῳ ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ ὦ Σώκρατες Ἀλλὰ μὴν ἀφρονεστέρα γε ἡ τούτου ἢ ἡ τοῦ ἑτέρου
greek_tokens: o en o enantios egw dokew eimi swkraths alla meis afrwn ge o outos h o o eteros karteria alhqhs legw kai o meta episthmh ara ippikos karterew en ippomaxia hsswn fhmi andreios eimi h o aneu episthmh egw dokew kai o meta sfendonhtikos h tocikos h allos tis texnh karterew
english_head: The man opposed to him, I should say, Socrates. But yet his endurance is more foolish than that of the first man. That is true. So you would say that he who in a cavalry fight endures with a knowledge of horsemanship is less courageous than he who endures without it. Yes, I think so.

greek_head: Πάνυ γε Καὶ ὅσοι ἂν ἐθέλωσιν εἰς φρέαρ καταβαίνοντες καὶ
greek_tokens: panu ge kai osos an eqelw eis frear katabainw kai kolumbaw karterew en outos o ergon mh eimi deinos h en tis allos toioutos andreios fhmi o outos deinazw tis gar an tis allos fhmi eimi swkraths oudeis eiper oiomai ge outws alla meis oimh ge kai meis pou afrwn ge eimi laxh e toioutos kinduneuw te kai karterew h e meta texnh autos prassw fainw
english_head: And he who endures with a skill in slinging or shooting or other such art. To be sure. And anyone who agrees to descend into a well, and to dive, and to endure in this or other such action, without being an adept in these things, you would say is more courageous than the adepts. Yes, for what else can one say, Socrates? Nothing, provided one thinks so. But I do think it. And you observe, I suppose, Laches, that persons of this sort are more foolish in their risks and endurances than those who do

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν αἰσχρὰ ἡ ἄφρων τόλμα τε καὶ καρτέρησις ἐν τῷ πρόσθεν ἐφάνη ἡμῖν οὖσα καὶ βλαβερά
greek_tokens: oukoun aisxros o afrwn tolma te kai karterhsis en o prosqen fainw egw eimi kai blaberos panu ge o de ge andreia omologew kalos tis eimi omologew gar nun de au palin fhmi ekeinos o aisxros o afrwn karterhsis andreia eimi eoika kalos oun su dokew legw ma o diw eimi swkraths egw men ou ou ara pou kata o sos logos dwristi armozw
english_head: Now, we found before that foolish boldness and endurance are base and hurtful? Quite so. But courage was admitted to be something noble. Yes, it was. Whereas now, on the contrary, we say that this base thing—foolish endurance—is courage. Apparently. Then do you think our statement is correct? On my word, Socrates, not I. Hence I presume that, on your showing, you and I, Laches,

greek_head: ἐγώ τε καὶ σύ ὦ Λάχης τὰ γὰρ ἔργα οὐ συμφωνεῖ ἡμῖν τοῖς λόγοις ἔργῳ μὲν γάρ ὡς ἔοικε φαίη ἄν τις
greek_tokens: egw te kai su eimi laxh o gar ergon ou sumfwnew egw o logos ergon men gar ws eoika fhmi an tis egw andreia metexw logos de ws oimh ou an ei nun egw akouw dialegw alhqhs legw tis oun dokew kalos eimi outws egw diakeimai oude opwsoun boulomai oun os legw peiqw o ge tosoutos o poios dh outos kai tis outos
english_head: are not tuned to the Dorian harmony: for our deeds do not accord with our words. By our deeds, most likely, the world might judge us to have our share of courage, but not by our words, I fancy, if they should hear the way we are talking now. That is very true. Well now, does it seem right that we should be in such a condition? Not by any means. Then do you mind if we accept our statement to a certain point? To what point do you mean, and what statement?

greek_head: Τῷ λόγῳ ὃς καρτερεῖν κελεύει εἰ οὖν βούλει καὶ ἡμεῖς ἐπὶ τῇ ζητήσει ἐπιμείνωμέν τε καὶ καρτερήσωμεν ἵνα
greek_tokens: o logos os karterew keleuw ei oun boulomai kai egw epi o zhthsis epimenw te kai karterew ina kai mh egw autos o andreia katagelaw ostis ou andreios autos zhtew ei ara pollakis autos o karterhsis eimi andreia egw men etoimos eimi swkraths mh proafistamai kaitoi ahqhs ge eimi o toioutos logos alla tis egw kai filoneikia lambanw pros o erw kai ws alhqhs aganaktew
english_head: That which enjoins endurance. And, if you please, let us too be steadfast and enduring in our inquiry, so as not to be ridiculed by courage herself for failing to be courageous in our search for her, when we might perchance find after all that this very endurance is courage. For my part I am ready, Socrates, to continue without faltering; and yet I am unaccustomed to discussions of this sort. But a certain ambitious ardour has got hold of me at hearing what has been said,

greek_head: εἰ οὑτωσὶ ἃ νοῶ μὴ οἷός τ' εἰμὶ εἰπεῖν νοεῖν μὲν γὰρ ἔμοιγε δοκῶ περὶ ἀνδρείας ὅτι ἔστιν οὐκ οἶδα δ' ὅπῃ
greek_tokens: ei outwsi o noew mh oios su eimi eipon noew men gar egw dokazw peri andreia ostis eimi ou oida de oph egw arti diafeugw wste mh sullambanw o logos autos kai eipon ostis eimi oukoun eimi filos o agaqos kunhgeths metaqew xrh kai mh aneimi pantapasi men oun boulomai oun kai nikias ode parakalew epi o kunhgesion ei tis egw euporos eimi
english_head: and I am truly vexed at finding myself unable to express offhand what I think. For I feel that I conceive in thought what courage is, but somehow or other she has given me the slip for the moment, so that I fail to lay hold of her in speech and state what she is. Well, my dear sir, the good huntsman must follow the hounds and not give up the chase. Yes, indeed, by all means. Then do you agree to our inviting Nicias here to join in our hunt? He may be more resourceful than we are.

greek_head: Βούλομαι πῶς γὰρ οὔ Ἴθι δή ὦ Νικία ἀνδράσι φίλοις χειμαζομένοις ἐν
greek_tokens: boulomai pws gar ou eimi dh eimi nikias anhr filos xeimazw en logos kai aforaw bohqew ei tis exis dunamis o men gar dh hmeteros oraw ws aporos su de eipon ostis hgeomai andreia eimi egw te o aporia ekluw kai autos o noew o logos bebaiow dokew toinun egw pala ou kalos eimi swkraths orizw o andreia o gar egw sew hdh kalos legw akouw outos ou xraomai poios dh eimi nikias
english_head: I agree, of course. Come now, Nicias, and use what powers you have to assist your friends, who are caught in a storm of argument and are quite perplexed. You see the perplexity of our case; you must now tell us what you think courage is, and so at once set us free from our perplexity and give your own thoughts the stability of speech. Well, for some time I have been thinking, Socrates, that you two are not defining courage in the right way; for you are not acting upon an admirable remark which I

greek_head: Πολλάκις ἀκήκοά σου λέγοντος ὅτι ταῦτα ἀγαθὸς ἕκαστος ἡμῶν ἅπερ σοφός ἃ δὲ ἀμαθής ταῦτα δὲ κακός
greek_tokens: pollakis akouw su legw ostis outos agaqos ekastos egw o sofos o de amaqhs outos de kakos alhqhs mentoi nh dios legw eimi nikias oukoun eiper o andreios agaqos dhlos ostis sofos eimi akouw eimi laxh egw kai ou sfodra ge manqanw o legw alla egw dokazw manqanw kai egw dokew anhr sofia tis o andreia legw poa eimi swkraths sofia
english_head: I have often heard you say that every man is good in that wherein he is wise, and bad in that wherein he is unlearned. Well, that is true, Nicias, I must say. And hence, if the brave man is good, clearly he must be wise. Do you hear him, Laches? I do, without understanding very well what he says. But I think I understand it: our friend appears to me to mean that courage is a kind of wisdom. What kind of wisdom, Socrates?

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν τόνδε τοῦτο ἐρωτᾷς Ἔγωγε
greek_tokens: oukoun ode outos erwtaw egw eimi dh autos eipon eimi nikias poa sofia andreia an eaw kata o sos logos ou gar pou o ge aulhtikos oudamos oude meis o kiqaristikos ou dhta alla tis dh outos h tis episthmh panu men oun orqos autos erwtaw eimi swkraths kai eipon ge tis fhmi autos eimi outos egw eimi laxh o o deinazw kai qarsaleos
english_head: Well, will you put that question to your friend here? I do. Come now, tell him, Nicias, what kind of wisdom courage may be, by your account. Not that, I presume, of flute-playing. Not at all. Nor yet that of harping. Oh, no. But what is this knowledge then, or of what? I must say you question him quite correctly, Socrates, so let him just tell us what he thinks it is. I say, Laches, that it is this—the knowledge of what is to be dreaded or dared,

greek_head: ἐπιστήμην καὶ ἐν πολέμῳ καὶ ἐν τοῖς ἄλλοις ἅπασιν Ὡς ἄτοπα λέγει ὦ Σώκρατες
greek_tokens: episthmh kai en polemos kai en o allos apas ws atopos legw eimi swkraths pros tis outos eipon blepw eimi laxh pros ostis xwris dhpou sofia eimi andreia oukoun fhmi ge nikias ou mentoi ma dios outos su kai lhrew oukoun didaskw autos alla mh loidorew ou alla egw dokew eimi swkraths laxh epiqumew amos fainw mhdeis legw ostis kai autos arti toioutos
english_head: either in war or in anything else. How strangely he talks, Socrates! What is it that makes you say that, Laches? What is it? Why, surely wisdom is distinct from courage. Well, Nicias denies that. He does indeed, to be sure: that is where he just babbles. Then let us instruct and not abuse him. No, it seems to me, Socrates, that Laches wants to have it proved that I am talking nonsense, because he was proved

greek_head: τις ἐφάνη Πάνυ μὲν οὖν ὦ Νικία καὶ πειράσομαί γε ἀποφῆναι
greek_tokens: tis fainw panu men oun eimi nikias kai peiraw ge apofainw oudeis gar legw epei autika en o nosos ou e iatros o deinos efisthmi h e andreios dokew su efisthmi h o iatros su andreios kalew oude opwsoun oude ge o gewrgos oimh kaitoi o ge en o gewrgia deinos outos dhpou efisthmi kai e allos dhmiourgos apas o en o eautou texnh deinos te kai
english_head: a moment ago to be in the same case himself. Quite so, Nicias, and I will try to make it evident. You are talking nonsense: for instance, do not doctors know what is to be dreaded in disease? Or do you suppose that the courageous know this? Or do you call doctors courageous No, not at all. Nor, I fancy, farmers either. And yet they, I presume, know what is to be dreaded in farming, and every other skilled worker knows what is to be dreaded and dared in his own craft; but they are none the more

greek_head: θαρραλέα ἴσασιν ἀλλ' οὐδέν τι μᾶλλον οὗτοι ἀνδρεῖοί εἰσιν
greek_tokens: qarsaleos oida alla oudeis tis mallon outos andreios eimi tis dokew laxh legw eimi nikias eoika mentoi legw tis kai gar legw ge tis ou mentoi alhqhs ge pws dh ostis oiomai o iatros pleos tis oida peri o kamnw h o ugieinos eipon oios te kai noswdhs e de dhpou tosoutos monos oida ei de deinos tis outos eimi o ugiainw mallon h o kamnw hgeomai su outos eimi laxh o iatros efisthmi h ou polus oiomai ek o nosos ameinwn eimi mh anisthmi h anisthmi outos gar eipon
english_head: courageous for that. What is Laches saying, in your opinion, Nicias? There does seem to be something in it. Yes, there is something, only it is not true. How so? Because he thinks that doctors know something more, in treating sick persons, than how to tell what is healthy and what diseased. This, I imagine, is all that they know: but to tell whether health itself is to be dreaded by anyone rather than sickness, —do you suppose, Laches, that this is within a doctor’s knowledge? Do you not think t

greek_head: σὺ πᾶσι φῂς ἄμεινον εἶναι ζῆν καὶ οὐ πολλοῖς κρεῖττον τεθνάναι
greek_tokens: su pas fhmi ameinwn eimi zaw kai ou polus kreisswn qnhskw oimh egw outos ge os oun qnhskw lusitelew tautos oiomai deinos eimi kai os zaw ou egw alla outos dh su didwmi o iatros gignwskw h allos tis dhmiourgos plhn o o deinazw kai mh deinazw episthmwn os egw andreios kalew katanoew eimi laxh ostis legw
english_head: that they should never arise from their bed of sickness? Pray tell me, do you say that in every case it is better to live? Is it not often preferable to be dead? I do think that is so. And do you think that the same things are to be dreaded by those who were better dead, as by those who had better live? No, I do not. Well, do you attribute the judgement of this matter to doctors or to any other skilled worker except him who has knowledge of what is to be dreaded and what is not—the man whom I ca

greek_head: Ἔγωγε ὅτι γε τοὺς μάντεις καλεῖ τοὺς ἀνδρείους τίς γὰρ δὴ ἄλλος εἴσεται ὅτῳ ἄμεινον ζῆν ἢ τεθνάναι
greek_tokens: egw ostis ge o mantis kalew o andreios tis gar dh allos eimi ostis ameinwn zaw h qnhskw kaitoi su eimi nikias poteros omologew mantis eimi h oute mantis oute andreios tis de mantis au oiomai proshkw o deinos gignwskw kai o qarsaleos egw tis gar allos os egw legos polus mallon eimi beltistos epei mantis ge o shmeion monos deomai gignwskw o eimi eite tis qanatos eite nosos eite apobolh xrhma eimi
english_head: I do: it seems to be the seers whom he calls the courageous: for who else can know for which of us it is better to be alive than dead? And yet, Nicias, do you avow yourself to be a seer, or to be neither a seer nor courageous? What! Is it now a seer, think you, who has the gift of judging what is to be dreaded and what to be dared? That is my view: who else could it be? Much rather the man of whom I speak, my dear sir: for the seer’s business is to judge only the signs of what is yet to come—whe

greek_head: εἴτε νίκη εἴτε ἧττα ἢ πολέμου ἢ καὶ ἄλλης τινὸς ἀγωνίας ὅτι δέ τῳ ἄμεινον τούτων ἢ παθεῖν ἢ μὴ παθεῖν τί μᾶλλον
greek_tokens: eite nikh eite hssa h polemos h kai allos tis agwnia ostis de tis ameinwn outos h pasxw h mh pasxw tis mallon mantis proshkw krinw h allos ostis alla egw outos ou manqanw eimi swkraths ostis boulomai legw oute gar mantis oute iatros oute allos oudeis dhlow ostis legw o andreios ei mh ei qeos tis legw autos eimi egw men oun fainw nikias ou eqelw
english_head: or victory or defeat in war or some other contest; but what is better among these things for a man to suffer or avoid suffering, can surely be no more for a seer to decide than for anyone else in the world. Well, I fail to follow him, Socrates, or to see what he is driving at; for he points out that neither a seer nor a doctor nor anybody else is the man he refers to as the courageous, unless perchance he means it is some god. Now it appears to me

greek_head: γενναίως ὁμολογεῖν ὅτι οὐδὲν λέγει ἀλλὰ στρέφεται ἄνω καὶ κάτω ἐπικρυπτόμενος τὴν αὑτοῦ ἀπορίαν καίτοι κἂν
greek_tokens: gennaios omologew ostis oudeis legw alla strefw anoos kai katos epikruptw o eautou aporia kaitoi kan egw oios te eimi arti egw te kai su toioutos strefw ei boulomai mh dokew enantios egw autos legw ei men oun en dikasthrion egw e logos eimi exw an tis logos outos poiew nun de tis an tis en sunousia toiosde math kenos logos autos eautou kosmew
english_head: that Nicias is unwilling to admit honestly that he has no meaning at all, but dodges this way and that in the hope of concealing his own perplexity. Why, you and I could have dodged in the same way just now, if we wished to avoid the appearance of contradicting ourselves. Of course, if we were arguing in a law-court, there would be some reason for so doing; but here, in a meeting like this of ours, why waste time in adorning oneself with empty words? I agree that it is out of place, Laches: but

greek_head: Οὐδὲν οὐδ' ἐμοὶ δοκεῖ ὦ Λάχης ἀλλ' ὁρῶμεν μὴ Νικίας οἴεταί τι λέγειν καὶ οὐ λόγου ἕνεκα ταῦτα λέγει
greek_tokens: oudeis oude egw dokew eimi laxh alla oraw mh nikias oiomai tis legw kai ou logos eneka outos legw autos oun safhs puqw tis pote noew kai ean tis fainw legos sugxwrew ei de mh didaskw su toinun eimi swkraths ei boulomai punqanomai punqanomai egw de isos ikanos punqanomai alla oudeis egw kwluw koinos gar eimi o pustis uper egw te kai sew panu men oun legos dh egw eimi nikias mallon de egw koinow
english_head: perhaps Nicias thinks he does mean something, and is not talking just for the sake of talking. So let us ask him to explain more clearly what is in his mind; and if we find that he means something, we will agree with him; if not, we will instruct him. Then, Socrates, if you would like to ask him, please do so: I daresay I have done enough asking. Well, I see no objection, since the question will be on behalf of us both. Very well, then. Now tell me, Nicias, or rather, tell us—for Laches and I ar

greek_head: γὰρ ἐγώ τε καὶ Λάχης τὸν λόγον τὴν ἀνδρείαν ἐπιστήμην φῂς δεινῶν τε καὶ θαρραλέων εἶναι
greek_tokens: gar egw te kai laxh o logos o andreia episthmh fhmi deinazw te kai qarsaleos eimi egw outos de ou pas dh eimi anhr gignwskw opote ge mhte iatros mhte mantis autos gignwskw mhde andreios eimi ean mh autos outos o episthmh proslambanw ou outws legw outws men oun kata o paroimia ara o eimi ou an pas us gignwskw oude an andreia gignomai ou egw dokew
english_head: of what is to be dreaded or dared? I do. And that it is not every man that knows it, since neither a doctor nor a seer can know it, and cannot be courageous unless he add this particular knowledge to his own? This was your statement, was it not? Yes, it was. And so in fact this is not a thing which, as the proverb says, any pig would know; and thus a pig cannot be courageous. I think not.

greek_head: Δῆλον δή ὦ Νικία ὅτι οὐδὲ τὴν Κρομμυωνίαν ὗν πιστεύεις σύ γε ἀνδρείαν γεγονέναι τοῦτο δὲ λέγω οὐ
greek_tokens: dhlos dh eimi nikias ostis oude o krommuwnian us pisteuw su ge andreia gignomai outos de legos ou paizw alla anagkaion oimh o outos legw mhdeis qhrion apodexomai andreia h sugxwrew qhraw tis outws sofos eimi wste o oligos anqrwpos oida dia o xalepos eimi gignwskw outos lewn h pardalis h tis kapros fainw oida alla anagkh omoios lewn kai elafos kai tauros kai piqhkos pros andreia fainw fuw o tiqhmi andreia outos osper su tiqhmi
english_head: Indeed it is obvious, Nicias, that you at least do not believe that even the Crommyonian sow could have been courageous. I say this not in jest, but because I conceive it is necessary for him who states this theory to refuse courage to any wild beast, or else to admit that a beast like a lion or a leopard or even a boar is so wise as to know what only a few men know because it is so hard to perceive. Why, he who subscribes to your account of courage must needs agree that a lion, a stag, a bull,

greek_head: Νὴ τοὺς θεούς καὶ εὖ γε λέγεις ὦ Σώκρατες καὶ ἡμῖν ὡς ἀληθῶς τοῦτο ἀπόκριναι ὦ Νικία πότερον σοφώτερα
greek_tokens: nh o qeos kai eu ge legw eimi swkraths kai egw ws alhqhs outos apokrinw eimi nikias poteros sofos fhmi egw outos eimi o qhrion o pas omologew andreia eimi h pas enantioomai tolmaw mhde andreia autos kalew ou gar tis eimi laxh egw andreia kalew oute qhrion oute allos oudeis o o deinos upo anoia mh fobew alla afobos kai mwros h kai o paidia pas oiomai egw
english_head: Heavens, Socrates, how admirably you argue! Now answer us sincerely, Nicias, and say whether those animals, which we all admit to be courageous, are wiser than we are; or whether you dare, in contradiction of everyone else, describe them as not even courageous. No, Laches, I do not describe animals, or anything else that from thoughtlessness has no fear of the dreadful, as courageous, but rather as fearless and foolish. Or do you suppose I describe all children

We hold that the dreadful are things that cause fear, and the safely ventured are those that do not; and fear is caused not by past or present, but by expected evils: for fear is expectation of coming evil. english_head: Yes, entirely so, Socrates. So there you have our view, Nicias,—that coming evils are to be dreaded, and things not evil, or good things, that are to come are to be safely dared. Would you describe them in this way, or in some other? I would describe them in this way. And the knowledge of these things is what you term courage? Precisely. There is still a third point on which we must see if you are in agreement with us.
